coredata.dc:title: A bimodal electrochemiluminescence method based on dual-enhancement Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system combined with magnetic field enhanced solid-phase microextraction for the direct determination of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Luyao
Mao, Jie
Wang, Sui
Hu, Yufang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the paper developed a bimodal electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method based on dual-enhancement Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system combined with magnetic field enhanced solid-phase microextraction (MFE-SPME) for the direct determination of ascorbic acid (AA). The nanocomposite of Fe3O4@CS@GO, as a magnetic adsorbent, could efficiently extract AA under the external magnetic field provided by the magnetic glassy carbon electrode (MGCE) with the extraction efficiency up to 89.2%. Based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the co-reactant for Ru(bpy)3 2+, when the potential was swept cyclically between −3.5 and 2.0 V, the bimodal ECL emissions were obtained in the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system and were enhanced as AA concentration increased. The method could quantitatively detect AA, because the ln(ECL-1/ECL-2) of bimodal ECL system was in a good linear relationship with the negative logarithm of the AA concentration in the range of 10−5–10−9 M with the limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) of 330 pM. After a series of evaluations, the method exhibited good sensitivity, satisfactory repeatability and excellent anti-interference, demonstrating that the developed method had great application potential in analysis fields.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: ECL intensities of three different CQDs. Three different CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using β-Alanine (purple line), Iminodiacetic acid (red line) and N,N-Dimethylglycine (dark cyan line) as three different raw materials.

(A) TEM image of CQDs. Inset: TEM image of CQDs with higher magnification; (B) Size distribution histogram of CQDs; (C) The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs; (D) XPS spectrum of CQDs; (E) FL spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths；(F) UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectra of CQDs, λEx = 320 nm and λEm = 394 nm in the figure. Inset: The photographs of CQDs under natural light (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right).
SEM images of (A) Fe3O4; (B) Fe3O4@CS; (C) Fe3O4@CS@GO.

(A) and (B) ECL behaviors of (a) Ru(bpy)3 2+, (b) CQDs, (c) AA, (d) Ru(bpy)3 2+/AA, (e) Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs and (f) Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA. In addition, Fig. 4B is a partially magnifying diagram of Fig. 4A. (C) Cyclic Voltammograms of (a) Ru(bpy)3 2+, (b) CQDs, (c) AA, (d) Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs and (e) Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA. (D) Enlargement of Cyclic Voltammograms with a black rectangle in Fig. 4C. The optimal experimental conditions: 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS buffer solution, 1 mg/mL CQDs, 0.1 mM Ru(bpy)3 2+, 10−5 M AA and the scanning rate of 0.3 V/s.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) The FL spectra of (a) Ru(bpy)3 2+, (b) CQDs, (c) AA, (d) Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs, (e) Ru(bpy)3 2+/AA, (f) CQDs/AA and (g) Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA.

The ECL intensities of (A) ECL-1 (I 1) and (B) ECL-2 (I 2) under the different extraction conditions: (a) the unextracted 10−6 M AA standard solution; (b) magnetic field solid-phase microextraction; (c) solid-phase microextraction. (C) The extraction efficiency of different magnetic adsorbents ((a) Fe3O4@CS; (b) Fe3O4@GO; (c) Fe3O4@CS@GO) with the magnetic field. (D) The extraction efficiency of Fe3O4@CS@GO with or without the magnetic field.

(A) ECL intensity stereogram with different concentrations of AA (1 nM, 10 nM, 100 nM, 1 μM and 10 μM). (B) The linear relationship between I 1 (dark cyan line) or I 2 (red line) and the negative logarithm of the AA concentration in the range of 10−5– 10−9 M. (C) The linear calibration curve for AA determination under optimal conditions.

(A) The selectivity of the method for 10−7 M AA against various interferences (100.000-fold concentrations of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2− and CO3 2−, and 10,000-fold concentrations of ethanol, glucose, sucrose and lysine). (B) The anti-interference performance for the detection of AA (coexisting substances in 10−7 M AA including 100,000-fold concentrations of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2− and CO3 2−, and 10,000-fold concentrations of ethanol, glucose, sucrose and lysine). (C) The bimodal ECL responses of continuous cyclic voltammetry scans for 8 cycles.
Schematic illustration of the experimental procedure.
Schematic illustration of mechanism of bimodal ECL for AA detection.
Results of the recovery of AA concentration in orangeade.

head.ce:title: A bimodal electrochemiluminescence method based on dual-enhancement Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system combined with magnetic field enhanced solid-phase microextraction for the direct determination of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, State Key Laboratory Base of Novel Functional Materials and Preparation Science, School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, State Key Laboratory Base of Novel Functional Materials and Preparation Science, School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, People'’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the paper developed a bimodal electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method based on dual-enhancement Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system combined with magnetic field enhanced solid-phase microextraction (MFE-SPME) for the direct determination of ascorbic acid (AA). The nanocomposite of Fe3O4@CS@GO, as a magnetic adsorbent, could efficiently extract AA under the external magnetic field provided by the magnetic glassy carbon electrode (MGCE) with the extraction efficiency up to 89.2%. Based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the co-reactant for Ru(bpy)3 2+, when the potential was swept cyclically between −3.5 and 2.0 V, the bimodal ECL emissions were obtained in the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system and were enhanced as AA concentration increased. The method could quantitatively detect AA, because the ln(ECL-1/ECL-2) of bimodal ECL system was in a good linear relationship with the negative logarithm of the AA concentration in the range of 10−5–10−9 M with the limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) of 330 pM. After a series of evaluations, the method exhibited good sensitivity, satisfactory repeatability and excellent anti-interference, demonstrating that the developed method had great application potential in analysis fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new photoluminescent nanomaterial, was found accidentally by Xu et al. in the process of separating and purifying single walled carbon nanotubes by the method of arc discharge [1] for the first time in 2004. CQDs is expected as a powerful tool for biomedicine because of its exceptional characteristics including excellent chemical stability, hypotoxicity, satisfactory biocompatibility, small size, highly tunable emission and so on [2–4], which is widely applied in the various fields such as bioimaging, targeted drug delivery [5,6], photocatalysis [7] and biosensors [8–14]. In recent years, CQDs becomes a promising material for ECL co-reactant, which has received widespread attention [15–19].

Ascorbic acid (AA) is also known as Vitamin C, which is an indispensable water-soluble vitamin in the human body, and which plays an important role in regulating various redox metabolic reactions. AA naturally exists in various agricultural products such as vegetables and fruits, and cannot be produced directly by the human body itself. In addition, AA, as an antioxidant was widely used in pharmaceutical, food, biological and other fields, helps lower blood pressure in patients with hypertension, plays the important roles in preventing neurodegenerative diseases and cancer caused by free radicals, and can also effectively prevent common cold, scurvy and so on [20–22]. Researches found that deficiency of AA results in serious diseases such as anemia [23] and mental illness [24], but excessive intake of AA may cause diarrhea, urinary stones and stomach cramps [25]. Therefore, the development of an accurate and sensitive method for the detection of AA is of great significance for food security and medical detection [26]. Up to now, various traditional methods for AA detection have been designed, including high performance liquid chromatography [27–29], colorimetry [30,31], fluorescence spectroscopy [32] and electrophoresis [33]. However, these detection methods are limited to some extent due to the shortcomings of expensive equipment, complicated technology and consuming time. As we all know, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method has attracted wide attention because of its advantages such as high sensitivity, fast response, low cost for equipment and simple operation, which is an essential method for detecting AA in real samples.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), as a practical analytical technique, combines the advantages of electrochemical and chemiluminescence methods including high sensitivity and low background, which has been used widely in various research fields. ECL analysis generally uses a three-electrode system, which applies a certain voltage to the chemical system containing the luminophore through the working electrode, causing that chemical substances gain or lose electrons in the solution. And the excited state of the luminophor is formed during the electrochemical reactions [34]. When the luminophor returns from the excited state to the ground state, the energy is released with the form of light. The production of ECL is mainly explained by two reaction mechanisms. One is the ion annihilation reaction mechanism at the double-step alternating positive and negative pulse potentials [35]. When the alternating potentials are applied to the electrode surface, the electroactive materials are oxidized at the anode and reduced at the cathode to obtain radical cations and radical anions, respectively. Above two free radical ions react with each other to produce the excited state and emit ECL. The other is co-reactant electrochemiluminescence reaction mechanism. ECL emission is generated by the introduction of the co-reactant in the presence of the electroluminescent reagent under the unidirectional potential. The co-reactant can become the intermediate species with the strong reducibility or oxidizability when the oxidation or reduction reaction occurs. And the produced intermediate species unceasingly react with the electroluminescent agent to engender the excited state molecule. Therefore, the selection of efficient co-reactants is extremely necessary for improving the performance of ECL. Tris(2,2′-bipyridine)dichlororuthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) as a typical ECL emitter has been extensively applied in highly sensitive ECL sensors [36–38] because of its good electrochemical stability, satisfactory luminescent efficiency and favorable sensitivity [39,40]. Although Tripropylamine (TPA) is a typical co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+, which is limited in the application of sensors due to the volatility, corrosivity and toxicity [41]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to exploit a new alternative co-reactant which is expected to be low-cost, environmentally friendly and nontoxic. The low-toxicity CQDs is preferred [42,43]. For instance, CQDs was reported for the first time by Long et al. [19] as an efficient and nontoxic co-reactant for Ru(bpy)3 2+. Li et al. [44] had also demonstrated that nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) could effectively enhance the ECL intensity. And Cola et al. utilized amine-rich N-CDs successfully as a novel co-reactant to facilitate ECL emission [41]. Most researches about ECL systems were based on the single signal of ECL, which would lead to false positive analysis results [45] because of the interference by external factors. In order to improve the reliability of the analysis results, the ratio ECL sensor is proposed using a linear relationship between the ratio of dual ECL signals and the concentration of the target analyte. Xu et al. [46], Hu et al. [47] and Wang et al. [35] had constructed the ratio ECL sensor using two ECL emitters or two co-reactants. However, the bimodal ECL system with single ECL emitter and single co-reactant can reduce environmental pollution and save experimental costs, which is very necessary for the development of ECL sensors.

And how to extract AA from agricultural products or food is also a key technical issue which has been widely concerned. Extraction is widely used to extract heavy metal ion and organic compounds [48] in view of its excellent efficiency and simple process. Recently, magnetic field enhanced solid-phase microextraction (MFE-SPME), as a sample pretreatment technique, has attracted the attention of researchers [49], which is based on the target analytes being extracted by the magnetic adsorbent dispersed in the sample and separated by the external magnet. This technique not only has a fast and simple separation process, but also can improve extraction efficiency to a certain extent based on the differences of the magnetic susceptibility between the target analyte and the medium where it is dispersed [50,51]. In order to obtain higher extraction performance, the promising magnetic adsorbents are usually prepared by combining magnetic materials and adsorbent materials. As we all know, chitosan (CS) is a naturally-occurring biopolymer with abundant hydroxyl and amino groups in its molecule, which is an excellent adsorbent for heavy metal ions in aqueous solution and drug molecules in the environment. Graphene oxide (GO) is proven to be a satisfactory adsorbent [52,53] because it has a relatively large specific surface area and rich oxygen-containing groups including carboxyl, epoxy and hydroxyl groups [54–57]. However, as adsorbents, CS and GO have the drawbacks with the poor solubility and easy agglomeration, respectively, when they exist alone. Therefore, the magnetic composite material is prepared by combining CS, GO and Fe3O4, which will reduce the aggregation of GO, increase the solubility of CS and greatly improve extraction performance compared with each component. Since AA is a water-soluble target analyte, it can be successfully extracted by the magnetic adsorbent Fe3O4@CS@GO. According to the above introduction, it will be widely concerned if the bimodal ECL method utilizing CQDs as a co-reactant combines with MFE-SPME.

In this study, the magnetic adsorbent of Fe3O4@CS@GO was prepared to directly extract and separate AA under the external magnetic field from the magnetic glassy carbon electrode (MGCE) and the method of bimodal ECL in dual-enhancement Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system combined with MFE-SPME was designed for the direct determination of AA. When the potential was cyclically scanned between −3.5 and 2.0 V, the dual ECL signals were generated in the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system based on CQDs as the co-reactant. As AA concentration increased, the bimodal ECL intensities were improved correspondingly. Therefore, this method can be developed to accurately detect AA.

All reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade and were not further purified. Sodium hydroxide, aqueous ammonia (28% ~ 30%), hydrochloric acid and sodium acetate anhydrous were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris(2,2′-bipyridine)dichlororuthenium(II) hexahydrate was provided by ADA Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethanol, chitosan, graphene oxide, (±)-Epichlorohydrin, iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), ascorbic acid (AA), β-Alanine, iminodiacetic acid, N,N-Dimethylglycine, ethylene glycol and other reagents were bought from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. All experimental water used was ultrapure water.

Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M) used as electrolyte throughout the all electrochemical detection was obtained by mixing the standard solution of 0.1 M sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4) and 0.1 M sodium dihydngen phoshate anhydrous (NaH2PO 4 ). The different pH values of PBS can be adjusted by changing the different volume ratios of 0.1 M Na2HPO4 and 0.1 M NaH2PO 4 .

The electrochemiluminescence (ECL) experiments were completed using BPCL Ultra Weak Luminescence Analyzer (BPCL-1-TIC, Institute of Biophysics, CAS., Beijing, China) with the photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage of 800 V during the whole experiments. The electrochemical tests were performed with a CHI1110B electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode system including a MGCE as the working electrode, a platinum (Pt) wire as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl, 3 M) electrode as the reference electrode. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on UV–visible spectrometer of TU-1091 (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Ltd. Tokyo, Japan) recorded the FL spectra. The photos of the CQDs solution with fluorescence were taken under UV light of the hand-held ultraviolet lamp. FT-IR spectra were obtained on the Nexus 6700 Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet Corporation, USA). The size and morphology of samples were characterized by the H7650 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi, Ltd. Tokyo, Japan) or the SU-70 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Ltd. Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was acquired by the ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., America) with vacuum degree at 8 × l0−10 Pa and using Al Kα ray as excitation source. The magnetic hysteresis loop of material was measured via a Lakeshore7410 vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Lake Shore Cryotronics, Inc., USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was characterized by the laser nano particle size analyzer of Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Panalytical Ltd., England).

Three CQDs were synthesized using three different raw materials named β-Alanine, iminodiacetic acid and N,N-Dimethylglycine via one-step hydrothermal method [16]. Taking the CQDs synthesized from N,N-Dimethylglycine as an example, briefly, 0.3 g of N,N-Dimethylglycine was dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water to obtain a homogeneous dispersion under stirring. In the following step, the above solution was added into a Teflon-lined reaction kettle and heated at 200 °C for 3 h, and later cooled to room temperature. Then the colorless and transparent solution was obtained. The CQDs solution was obtained by centrifugation of the above colorless and transparent solution at 10,000 rpm for 15 min without any precipitate. The synthetic CQDs was stored in ultrapure water at 4 °C before using. The other two kinds of CQDs were prepared by the same process.

The Fe3O4@CS magnetic nanoparticles were prepared by solvothermal method based on the previous reports [58]. The specific steps were shown below: 0.82 g of anhydrous FeCl3 was added into 40 mL of ethylene glycol under vigorously stirring until the solution became uniform and transparent. Then 3.6 g of sodium acetate anhydrous and 1.0 g of chitosan were sequentially added into the mixed solution with stirring continuously for 30 min. Next, the above solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined reaction kettle and thermally treated at 200 °C for 12 h in a blast dryer, and subsequently cooled to room temperature. The black reaction product was separated and collected by a magnet, and washed with anhydrous ethanol for several times. Finally, the Fe3O4@CS magnetic nanoparticles were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for further use. In addition, the Fe3O4 magnetic nanomaterial was prepared by the same procedure [58], excepting chitosan was not added during the preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample pretreatment process and ECL detection
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe3O4@CS@GO magnetic nanocomposite as the magnetic adsorbent was used to extract AA by MFE-SPME. The sample pretreatment process was completed according to the previous literature with some modification [59]. Firstly, 10 mg of the magnetic nanocomposite Fe3O4@CS@GO was dispersed in the beaker containing 10 mL of AA standard solution of different concentrations or spiked samples, and the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 7. And the mixed solution was dispersed sufficiently by ultrasonic to make the magnetic adsorbent Fe3O4@CS@GO contact the analyte fully. For each extraction and ECL detection, the bare MGCE was polished to a mirror-like surface with 0.05, 0.3 and 1.0 μm α-Al2O3 polishing powders successively, and cleaned by ultrasonic with ultrapure water and ethanol, and then dried in nitrogen flow for further use. Then the clean MGCE providing the external magnetic field was inserted into the mixed solution, and the analyte was extracted on the magnetic adsorbent Fe3O4@CS@GO as much as possible by chemical adsorption force and magnetic field action until equilibrium. At the same time, the magnetic adsorbent was magnetically separated from the mixed solution to the MGCE rapidly. After extraction, the above MGCE with magnetic adsorbent was transferred to the electrolytic cell for electrochemical testing. The schematic illustration of the entire experimental procedure was shown in Scheme 1 .

The electrolytic cell contained 2 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 mM Ru(bpy)3 2+, 1 mg/mL CQDs and different concentrations of AA. The Cyclic Voltammogram and ECL intensity were recorded with a scanning rate of 0.3 V/s and a potential range from −3.5 to 2 V. 800 V was set as the voltage of the photomultiplier tube. In the ECL detection, I 1 (ECL-1) was obtained under anodic sweep, and I 2 (ECL-2) was obtained under cathodic sweep. All tests were repeated at least three times under the same conditions, and the results were obtained by calculating the average.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of ECL intensity of different CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three different CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using β-Alanine, iminodiacetic acid and N,N-Dimethylglycine as three different raw materials. The ECL intensities of the above three CQDs were studied, as shown in Fig. 1 . It could be observed that the ECL intensity of the CQDs synthesized from N, N-Dimethylglycine as raw material was the strongest and thus the above CQDs was selected as co-reactant for Ru(bpy)3 2+ in all tests and characterizations.

The size and morphology of the CQDs were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 2A. It could be found that the synthetic CQDs exhibited satisfactory dispersibility and nearly uniform spherical structure with the diameter between 3 and 8 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter of the CQDs was characterized by DLS. The size distribution histogram showed that the synthetic CQDs had a relatively concentrated size distribution in the range of 2.5 to 10 nm and an average diameter of 4.1 nm which was calculated according to Fig. 2B.

As shown in Fig. 2C, FT-IR spectrum was used to study the formation and molecular structure of CQDs. The characteristic peaks at 3420 cm−1 [60] and 3127 cm−1 [61] corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bonds, respectively. The characteristic absorption located at 2820 cm−1 with relatively weak intensity corresponded to the stretching vibration of the CH bond. The stretching vibrations of CO and C-OH in the carboxyl group were observed at 1632 cm−1 and 1404 cm−1 [61]. The characteristic peaks at 1283 cm−1 [61] and 1170 cm−1 were attributed to the CN stretching vibration and COH vibration in hydroxyl, respectively. The peak at 920 cm−1 corresponded to the OH bending vibration in carboxylic acid. These results revealed the presence of nitrogen and COOH structure at the synthetic CQDs.

XPS spectrum was obtained to analyze the chemical composition of CQDs as shown in Fig. 2D. The characteristic peaks located at 285.1 eV, 400.8 eV and 530.37 eV corresponded to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s [62,63], respectively. The result of the XPS survey spectrum demonstrated the existence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements with the relevant atomic ratios of 0.5736, 0.1326 and 0.2938 in CQDs, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum was deconvoluted into three main peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.4 eV and 287.3 eV, which were ascribed to CC [64], CN/CO [60] and CO bonds [62,65,66] (Fig. S1A), respectively. The deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectrum for N 1 s displayed two peaks at 398.6 eV and 400.8 eV [60], which could be attributed to the presence of CN and NH bonds (Fig. S1B) [65], severally. As shown in Fig. S1C, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s appeared two deconvoluted peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.5 eV, which were assigned to CO and COH bonds. These above results further proved the presence of carboxyl and amidogen.

Fig. 2E showed the emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity and emission wavelengths of CQDs both were changed with the increments of the excitation wavelengths from 240 nm to 480 nm. When the excitation wavelength was 320 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was the strongest and the corresponding emission wavelength was 394 nm. The results of the above analyses demonstrated an excitation-dependent emission behavior of CQDs.

In the Fig. 2F, UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibited the absorption peak at 287 nm. This might be caused by the n → π* transition of CO [67]. The excitation and emission spectra in the Fig. 2F indicated that the maximum excitation wavelength was 320 nm and the maximum emission wavelength was 394 nm. The inset of the Fig. 2F showed that the solution of synthetic CQDs was colorless and transparent under natural light (left), and the color of CQDs solution was converted to blue fluorescence after irradiation under 365 nm UV light (right).

The size and morphology of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CS and Fe3O4@CS@GO magnetic materials were characterized by SEM as shown in Fig. 3 . It could be seen from Fig. 3A that Fe3O4 particles exhibited the regular spherical structure with the rough and flawed surface and the uniform particle size with the average particle size of 280 nm. Fe3O4 particles were stack due to the slightly high concentration of synthetic particles probably. The SEM image of Fe3O4@CS particles was shown in Fig. 3B. We could find that the Fe3O4@CS particles also exhibited the uniform spherical structure with the average particle size of approximately 300 nm. In addition, Fe3O4@CS particles possessed the fuller surface with fewer defect than Fe3O4 particles. In Fig. 3C, it could be found that the layered structure of GO like gauze with the relatively large specific area covered on the surface of Fe3O4@CS particles which were stacked together. These above results indicated that Fe3O4@CS@GO magnetic adsorbent was synthesized successfully.

The formation of magnetic adsorbent was investigated by FT-IR spectra in Fig. S2A. It could be observed from Fig. S2A that the characteristic peak of Fe3O4 at 586 cm−1 was assigned to FeO stretching vibration [68]. For Fe3O4@CS, the absorption peaks at 3423 cm−1 [69] and 3162 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of OH bond and NH stretching vibration of NH2 in CS. In addition, the characteristic peaks at 2918 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1 were attributed to the CH antisymmetric stretching vibration and CH symmetrical stretching vibration of CH2 in CS, the peak located at 1624 cm−1 was generated by the in-plane deformation vibration of the NH bond in NH2 [70], and the absorption peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1074 cm−1 were ascribed to the deformation vibration of C-OH and the deformation vibration of C-O-C. Above absorption peaks for Fe3O4@CS were all characteristic absorption peaks of CS while the characteristic peak at 595 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of FeO, which could prove the successful synthesis of Fe3O4@CS. For Fe3O4@CS@GO, the stretching vibration of OH in CS and the NH stretching vibration of NH2 in CS were observed at 3409 cm−1 and 3145 cm−1, respectively. The CH antisymmetric stretching vibration and CH symmetrical stretching vibration of CH2 in CS appeared at 2929 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 1642 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO and the in-plane deformation vibration of NH in the amide group (NHCO) formed after the cross-linking of CS and GO. Additionally, the characteristic peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1072 cm−1 corresponded to the deformation vibration of C-OH and the deformation vibration of COC, respectively. The absorption peak at 596 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic peak of Fe3O4, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of FeO. The results of the above analyses indicated that Fe3O4@CS@GO was synthesized successfully. The magnetic hysteresis loops of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CS and Fe3O4@CS@GO were recorded via vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) in magnetic field strength range of ±10 kOe at 25 °C as shown in Fig. S2B. It could be easily found that the saturation magnetization values of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CS and Fe3O4@CS@GO were 84.1 emu/g, 31.9 emu/g and 14.2 emu/g, respectively. The reason for the decrease in the saturation magnetization values was the increase of the nonmagnetic components CQDs and GO in the synthetic magnetic nanomaterials. It could be observed from the magnetic hysteresis loops that residual magnetic flux density (Br) and the coercive force (Hc) were negligible, thus above magnetic nanomaterials possessed almost no hysteresis, illustrating that Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CS and Fe3O4@CS@GO had superparamagnetism. The superparamagnetic adsorbent materials could be attracted quickly by the magnet and could also be redispersed by ultrasonic after removing the magnetic field due to its hydrophilic characteristic. The inset in Fig. S2B intuitively verified the excellent magnetic performance of Fe3O4@CS@GO. In this work, the magnetic adsorbent Fe3O4@CS@GO could be completely concentrated on the MGCE under the applied magnetic field provided by MGCE, so the magnetic adsorbent could be completely separated from the aqueous solution and transferred to the electrolytic cell by MGCE. Therefore, there was basically no loss of the magnetic adsorbent and it would not affect the detection of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The electrochemiluminescence (ECL) behaviors and reaction mechanism of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL Behaviors of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the presence of different substances were investigated under the optimal experimental conditions (Figs. S3 and S4 in Supporting Information) in Fig. 4 . From Fig. 4A and B, we could observe that when Ru(bpy)3 2+ (curve a) or CQDs (curve b) existed in the electrolyte alone, the intensities of ECL peaks were extremely weak which could be ignored. When AA (curve c) existed in the electrolyte alone, no ECL peak was generated and the ECL intensity was almost zero. Additionally, the ECL peaks of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/AA system (curve d) were the same as the ECL peaks of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (curve a), indicating that AA did not interfere with ECL emissions of Ru(bpy)3 2+. Two distinct ECL peaks of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs system could be found in the presence of CQDs (curve e), which implied that the appearance of the bimodal ECL was caused probably by the interaction between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and CQDs in the electrochemical process. In addition, the intensities of bimodal ECL were increased obviously when AA was added to the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs system (curve f), which suggested that AA could enhance the ECL emissions of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs system. From Fig. 4C and D of the Cyclic Voltammograms (CV) corresponding to ECL behaviors, we could observe a pair of classic reversible redox peaks of Ru(bpy)3 2+ which corresponded to the oxidation-reduction of Ru(bpy)3 2+. The reduction peaks of Ru(bpy)3 2+, CQDs, AA, Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs and Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA were all around −0.71 V. It could also be found that the oxidation peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (curve a) was at about 1.28 V and that the oxidation peak current of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (curve a) was weak when Ru(bpy)3 2+ existed alone. However, the oxidation peak current of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs (curve d) increased significantly while the reduction peak current decreased, illustrating that the oxidation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ was catalyzed by CQDs [71,72] and further suggesting that CQDs could accelerate electrochemical reaction rate of Ru(bpy)3 2+, which was analogous to the role of co-reactant in ECL system [16,17]. With the above phenomenon of enhanced ECL intensities, we could preliminarily conclude that CQDs was used as the co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+. Moreover, the redox peaks of AA itself were exhibited in curve c of Fig. 4C and D, and we could also find that the oxidation peak current of AA was extremely weak. Nevertheless, the oxidation peak current of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs continued increasing when AA was introduced (curve e), which corresponded to the ECL behaviors. This phenomenon occurred probably because the interaction between AA and Ru(bpy)3 2+ or CQDs enhanced the bimodal ECL intensities of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs. The above results demonstrated the feasibility for detecting AA.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and FL spectra of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system were recorded to further study the mechanism of bimodal ECL for AA detection. Similar to previous research [19], the anodic electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the absence of co-reactant was particularly weak as also shown in Fig. 4A. It could be clearly found in Fig. 5A that the UV–vis absorption peaks (287 nm and 451 nm) of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA mixture were the merging of the absorption peaks of these three substances existing alone in the wavelength range of 220–800 nm, which illustrated that no new UV–vis absorption peak appeared and further proved that the interaction among the reactants was extremely weak and that no direct chemical reaction occurred among them and that no other compound was formed before the potential was applied. In addition, the FL spectra of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system were studied as shown in Fig. 5B. A fluorescence emission peak of CQDs appeared at the wavelength of 510 nm, while a strong fluorescence characteristic peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+ was generated at the wavelength of 609 nm, but almost no fluorescence emission peak of AA was shown in FL spectra. And it could be clearly seen that the FL spectra of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs mixture and the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA mixture displayed two fluorescence emission peaks at the wavelengths of 510 nm and 609 nm corresponding to the two characteristic peaks of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and CQDs, which indicated that there was no chemical reaction between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and CQDs in the ground state and that no other substance was produced. Thus, it could be inferred from the above phenomena that the electrochemical reaction would be the reason of the ECL behaviors of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system, and that the ECL behaviors were generated by the electrochemical reaction of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and CQDs, which further illustrated that Ru(bpy)3 2+ was the ECL luminophor and CQDs was defined as the co-reactant in this system [16].

Based on the above analyses, the possible mechanism of bimodal ECL was displayed in the Scheme 2 . Under cathodic sweep, the Ru(bpy)3 2+ and CQDs were electrochemically reduced to Ru(bpy)3 + and CQDs·- (Eqs. (1-a) and (2)), respectively. The Ru(bpy)3 + could react with strongly oxidative CQDs·-, which resulted in the formation of the excited state Ru(bpy)3 2+* (Eq. (3)). When Ru(bpy)3 2+* returned to Ru(bpy)3 2+ of the ground state (Eq. (4)), the strong cathodic ECL emission was generated. When AA was introduced, AA would react with Ru(bpy)3 2+ to generate more Ru(bpy)3 + (Eq. (1-b)), which could promote the subsequent reactions (Eqs. (3) and (4)) so as to enhance the ECL intensity. Under anodic sweep, the ground state Ru(bpy)3 2+ and CQDs were oxidized to Ru(bpy)3 3+ and CQDs·+ (Eqs. (5) and (6-a)) on the electrode surface by losing one-electron. Continuously, CQDs·+ was deprotonated to form strong reductive CQDs· (Eq. (6-a)) which reacted with Ru(bpy)3 3+ to produce Ru(bpy)3 2+* (Eq. (7)). When the excited state Ru(bpy)3 2+* returned to the ground state, the excess energy was released with the form of light (Eq. (8)). Similarly, when AA existed in the system, AA would react with the above CQDs·+ to obtain more CQDs· (Eq. (6-b)) which could then react with Ru(bpy)3 3+ to produce more excited state Ru(bpy)3 2+* to enhance ECL emission. The possible reaction mechanism of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system was shown below:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of external magnetic field on extraction efficiency [69]
ce:section.ce:para:
Meng Mei et al. [50] and Y. Moliner-Martínez et al. [51] found that applying the magnetic field during the extraction processes could improve the extraction efficiency. Therefore, we also studied the influence of the external magnetic field provided by the MGCE on the extraction efficiency. The extraction efficiency percentage of AA could be calculated by the following equation:
Extraction efficiency
%
=
CelVel CaqVaq
× 100
in which Cel, Vel, Caq and Vaq represented the concentration of AA in the electrolyte (M), the volume of the electrolyte (L), the concentration of AA in the sample aqueous solution (M) and the volume of the sample aqueous solution (L), respectively. And Cel was calculated by the linear regression equation.

Fig. 6A and B displayed the ECL intensities of ECL-1 (I 1) and ECL-2 (I 2) respectively under the different extraction conditions, where curve a was the unextracted 10−6 M AA standard solution, curve b was under the condition of magnetic field solid-phase microextraction and curve c was under the condition of solid-phase microextraction. It could be seen that when magnetic field solid-phase microextraction was performed in the 10−6 M AA standard solution, the I 1 and I 2 (curve b) both were weaker than the corresponding ECL intensities (curve a) of the 10−6 M AA standard solution. Because AA could enhance the ECL intensities with this method, above results suggested that AA had not been fully extracted from the standard solution. According to the calculations, the above extraction efficiency was obtained at approximately 89.2%. When the standard solution of 10−6 M AA was solid-phase extracted without applying the magnetic field and other conditions remained unchanged, the I 1 and I 2 (curve c) were weaker than those of the above two cases with the extraction efficiency of 68.9%. Fig. 6C exhibited the extraction efficiency of different magnetic adsorbents with the magnetic field. It could be seen that the extraction efficiency of Fe3O4@CS, Fe3O4@GO and Fe3O4@CS@GO were 75.3%, 76.5% and 89.2%, respectively, which showed that Fe3O4@CS@GO had the highest extraction efficiency. At the same time, Fig. 6D displayed the extraction efficiency of Fe3O4@CS@GO with or without the magnetic field. The results further illustrated that applying the magnetic field could improve the efficiency of solid-phase microextraction to a certain extent.

Under the optimal experimental conditions with electrolyte of pH 7.4, the scanning rate of 0.3 V/s, sample solution of pH 7 and 30 min extraction time (Figs. S3 and S4 in Supporting Information), the AA at different concentrations after extraction via the above extraction steps were quantitatively measured by the ECL detection. Fig. 7A visually showed that as the AA concentration increased, the bimodal ECL intensities both increased. The I 1, I 2 and ln(I 1/I 2) were taken as functions for the concentration of AA, where I 1 was the ECL intensity during the anodic sweep and I 2 was the ECL intensity during the cathodic sweep. As shown in Fig. 7B, I 1 and I 2 were both linearly dependent on the negative logarithm of AA concentration from 10−5 M to 10−9 M with the correlation coefficients of R 1 2 = 0.99389 and R 2 2 = 0.9896. It could also be found from the Fig. 7C that through linear fitting, the ln(I 1/I 2) of bimodal ECL system was in a good linear relationship with the negative logarithm of the AA concentration in the range of 10−5–10−9 M with the limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) of 330 pM. The linear regression equation was ln(I 1/I 2) = 0.17444 × (−lgc)-0.8582, and the correlation coefficient R 2 was 0.99831. Obviously, we could find that R 2 was higher than R 1 2 and R 2 2, which indicated that the ratio method of bimodal ECL system had higher accuracy. Therefore, the ratio method was chosen to accurately determine the concentration of AA. In order to further prove the superiority of proposed method, this method was compared with previously reported detection methods for AA in the literature (Supporting Information Table S1). It could be found that proposed method exhibited a wider linear detection range and a low limit of detection, which further demonstrated the feasibility of this method for detecting AA.

The excellent selectivity, anti-interference and stability of the proposed method played a key role in the accurate detection of AA in complex samples. The selectivity of the method was assessed via comparing the bimodal ECL intensities of 10−7 M AA and various interferences including 100,000-fold concentrations of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2− and CO3 2−, and 10,000-fold concentrations of ethanol, glucose, sucrose and lysine. As shown in Fig. 8A, the ECL intensities were not affected by interferences, which proved that the method had superior selectivity for AA determination.

Subsequently, we investigated the interference of coexisting substances in AA detection by adding 100,000-fold concentrations of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2− and CO3 2−, and 10,000-fold concentrations of ethanol, glucose, sucrose and lysine to 10−7 M AA and comparing the corresponding bimodal ECL intensities. It could be seen from the Fig. 8B that the ECL intensities were not significantly affected by the coexisting substances, which indicated that the method had the excellent anti-interference performance for the detection of AA.

The stability of the method was examined by recording the bimodal ECL responses of continuous cyclic voltammetry scans for 8 cycles in Fig. 8C. The bimodal signals of ECL-1 and ECL-2 were hardly changed, demonstrating the superior stability and repeatability of the method.

In order to examine the practical applicability of the method, the spiked recovery test was performed on actual samples. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the orangeade was selected as the actual sample for the spiked recovery test. As shown in Table 1 , the actual samples of orangeade were spiked with 5 × 10−6 M, 10−6 M, 5 × 10−7 M and 10−7 M AA, and the spiked recoveries were calculated from 87.2% to 104.0% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) from 3.12% to 4.71%, suggesting that the detection method could be applied to the actual quantification of AA in complex samples. Above results further illustrated that the method had excellent reproducibility and feasibility in actual samples.

A method was proposed by combining bimodal ECL of dual-enhancement Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system and MFE-SPME for detecting AA. The magnetic adsorbent of Fe3O4@CS@GO under the external magnetic field from the MGCE could efficiently extract AA and the extraction efficiency could be improved to 89.2%. When the potential was cyclically scanned between −3.5 and 2.0 V, the bimodal ECL emissions were produced in the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system based on CQDs as the co-reactant for Ru(bpy)3 2+. When AA concentration increased, the bimodal ECL intensities were improved correspondingly. In the analysis process, we found that the ln(ECL-1/ECL-2) of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/AA system was in a good linear relationship with the negative logarithm of the AA concentration in the range of 10−5–10−9 M with the limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) of 330 pM. Thus, the method was successfully applied to the detection of AA in real samples with the good accuracy, satisfactory repeatability and excellent anti-interference. The proposed method with the excellent extraction efficiency and ECL efficiency will possess great development potential in the field of medical analysis.
